title: Décisions du Conseil des Ministres 27 février 2026 - Maurice Info - Partager et informer en toute intégralité
text:
Les membres du Cabinet ont pris note des avancées dans le dossier des Chagos au niveau législatif au Royaume-Uni, ainsi que du fait que les Maldives ne reconnaissent la souveraineté sur ledit archipel, dont l’objection à l’accord de Maurice avec les Britanniques, des retombées du voyage du Premier Ministre à Artificial Intelligence (AI) Impact Summit en Inde, du paiement de Rs 170 millions pour l’acaht des 1.1 millions de doses du vaccin Johnson and Johnson COVID-19, de l’extension d’une année pour les Provisional Driving Licenses (Learner) qui allaient expirer le 2er mars 2026 entre autres.
1. Cabinet has taken note, with concern, that the legislative process with regard to the Diego Garcia Military Base and British-Indian Ocean Territory Bill in the United Kingdom (UK) has been delayed and will be activated as soon as an agreement between the UK and the United States is reached. Government is following-up closely on the matter.
Cabinet has further taken note of the recent stand taken by the Government of the Republic of Maldives whereby it no longer recognises the sovereignty of the Republic of Mauritius over the Chagos Archipelago and is now objecting to the UK-Mauritius Agreement. In view thereof, Cabinet has decided to immediately suspend all diplomatic relations with the Republic of Maldives.
Cabinet has also taken note that the injunction filed by the “BIOT Citizens” led by Mr Misley Mandarin has been called before the British-Indian Ocean Territory Administration (BIOTA) Court on Thursday 26 February 2026 and the Court has, on the undertaking of the UK Government, extended the stay of the Removal Order up to 13 March 2026, when the matter will be heard on its merits. The BIOTA Court has also acceded to a request by the UK to order that no new persons be transported on the Chagos Islands.
2. Cabinet has taken note of the outcome of the recent participation of the Prime Minister in the Artificial Intelligence (AI) Impact Summit, at the invitation of HE Shri Narendra Modi, Prime Minister of India, in New Delhi. The Mauritian delegation comprised the Minister of Information Technology, Communication and Innovation, the Mauritian High Commissioner in India, senior officials from the Prime Minister’s Office, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Regional Integration and International Trade, as well as the Chief Executive Officer of Mauritius Telecom. The Summit was on the theme “AI for All and Welfare for All” and witnessed the participation of some 20 Heads of State and Government.
During his address at the Leaders’ Plenary, the Prime Minister stressed the urgent need to ensure that the gap in AI divide does not widen between developed and developing countries and called for the transformative power of AI to be at the service of humanity at large. He urged for dedicated initiatives, including the development of an enabling environment to foster a level-playing field so that countries, particularly Small Island Developing States, do not lag behind. The Prime Minister enjoined leaders to consider implementing an equitable international governance framework that promotes safe and trusted use of AI tools. He highlighted the need to foster partnerships among nations so that AI works towards the common good and acts as a socio-economic enabler.
The Prime Minister took the opportunity of his participation in the AI Summit to promote the Special Economic Zone, to be set up at Côte d’Or, and which will be designed to attract investment and geared towards promoting the economy of the future, including the digital economy and AI. He emphasised that in line with the Digital Transformation Blueprint 2025-2029, Mauritius will accelerate its transition towards a high-income innovation driven and digitally enabled economy and is looking forward to entering into strategic partnerships with high-end companies in the field of AI, Data Centres, Cloud Infrastructure and Fintech.
The Prime Minister had a one-to-one meeting with Shri Narendra Modi, Prime Minister of India at the Hyderabad House in New Delhi and discussed several issues of bilateral, regional and multilateral interests, including:
(a) the forthcoming Indian Ocean Conference to be hosted by Mauritius from 10 to 12 April 2026;
(b) assistance in the Port Sector;
(c) the India-Mauritius Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement (DTAA). Prime Minister Modi assured of the continued stance of India of not taking any action that would undermine the benefits that Mauritius currently enjoys under the DTAA;
(d) the setting up of a Special Economic Zone at Côte d’Or; and
(e) the America India Connect Cable Connection project announced by the Chief Executive Officer of Google during the Summit.
The Prime Minister also met with Dr S. Jaishankar, External Affairs Minister of India who reiterated the readiness of India to enhance bilateral cooperation as per the outcomes of the official visit of Prime Minister Modi to Mauritius in March 2025 and the Prime Minister’s State Visit to India in September 2025.
In the margins of the Conference, the Prime Minister met with HE Mr Bharrat Jagdeo, Vice President of Guyana, Dr Kristalina Georgieva, Director General of the International Monetary Fund and Mr Natarajan Chandrasekaran, Chairman of the TATA Group of Companies.
The Minister of Information Technology, Communications and Innovation intervened in the ICT Minister’s Forum where he presented the AI Vision Strategy for Mauritius which is articulated on a fair, accountable, inclusive and responsible AI framework. He also advocated for AI safety through regulatory and governance mechanisms and stressed for institutional readiness and appropriate oversight structures. In the margins of the summit, the Minister of Information Technology, Communications and Innovation held bilateral meetings with, inter alia, the Minister of State for Electronics and IT, representatives of the Centre for Development of Telematics, Mistral AI, NVIDIA, Google, Indian Cybercrime Coordination Centre and VisionPlus.
3. Cabinet has taken note that the provision of Rs1.1 billion voted in the 2025-2026 Budget for the payment of passage benefits to public officers was exhausted by end November 2025. As a result, a significant number of applications for encashment of passage benefits have been kept in abeyance since December 2025. In order not to deprive public officers of their dues, Cabinet has agreed to:
(a) an additional budgetary provision of Rs50 million being made for the payment of passage benefits to public officers who had already submitted their applications by end-December 2025, as well as to new applicants, provided that these are for travel, medical and educational purposes supported by appropriate documentary evidence; and
(b) the deferment of payment of passage benefits for pending cases and new applications for purposes other than medical, educational, and travel to July 2026. Those who had already applied by end-December 2025 would be paid the passage benefits in July 2026 on the same terms as prior to the implementation of the 2026 PRB Report, i.e., they would be paid in full.
4. Cabinet has agreed to the Ministry of Health and Wellness effecting part payment of Rs170 million for the outstanding claim in relation to Johnson and Johnson COVID-19 vaccines to African Vaccine Acquisition Trust.
The Commitment Undertaking and the Participation Agreement signed by the previous regime in May 2021 for the procurement of around 1.1 Million doses of Johnson and Johnson COVID-19 vaccines created an obligation on Government for the payment of the total amount due and that there was no possibility of substitution. Only 439,200 doses of Johnson and Johnson COVID-19 vaccines have been procured and there was an outstanding balance of 691,200 doses which have never being procured but for which payment was still required. An outstanding amount USD5,421,697.41 (around Rs243 million) with interest compounded on a daily basis was due in respect of these 691,200 doses of Johnson and Johnson COVID-19 vaccines.
Cabinet further noted that an amount of USD5,999,040 (around Rs277 million) was also due in respect of 599,040 doses of Pfizer vaccines. The contract agreement had been signed with Pfizer Inc. and BioNTech Manufacturing in October 2022 for the procurement of 999,140 doses of Pfizer vaccines. Only 400,140 doses had been supplied in December 2022 and the payment for the outstanding balance is still due.
Cabinet has also taken note that, on the other hand the Russian authorities have agreed to waive an amount of USD11,243,500 (around Rs521 million) which was due for the Sputnik V vaccines.
Cabinet has noted, with concern, that no procurement procedures have been followed for the procurement of COVID-19 vaccines during that period. It is understood that the decision for the supply of vaccines was taken during the High Level Committee on COVID-19 chaired by the former Prime Minister. However, no records of such decisions are available.
5. Cabinet has agreed to the Ministry of Land Transport issuing drafting instructions to the Attorney-General’s Office to amend the Road Traffic (Amendment) Regulations 2021 so as to extend the moratorium on the expiry date of Provisional Driving Licenses (Learner) for autocycle/motorcycle, currently scheduled to lapse on 01 March 2026, as transitional relief pending broader reforms to the driving test framework. The validity period of the Provisional Driving Licences (Learner) will be extended for a period of one year.
6. Cabinet has taken note of the appointment of Mr Yogendranath Servansingh, Mr Noor-Ud-Diin Ruhomaun and Dr Guy Rivaltz Julien Quenette as members of the National Road Safety Council.
7. Cabinet has agreed to the introduction of the Optical Council (Amendment) Bill into the National Assembly. The object of the Bill is to amend the Optical Council Act 2021 so as to:
(a) empower the Optical Council of Mauritius to regulate, in addition to the profession of opticians, the profession of orthoptists;
(b) review the composition of the Optical Council of Mauritius with a view to addressing the anomaly with regard to public sector representation; and
(c) abolish the pre-registration examination for opticians as well as orthoptists.
8. Cabinet has agreed to the promulgation of the Higher Education (Use of Artificial Intelligence) Regulations 2026 which will apply to the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in the higher education sector in Mauritius. The regulations will confer upon the Higher Education Commission the authority and the duty to:
(a) issue guidelines, directives and standards to regulate the use of AI across all higher education institutions operating in Mauritius;
(b) prohibit uses of AI that may lead to bias, breach of academic integrity, plagiarism, or violation of data protection, intellectual property, copyright and cybersecurity laws;
(c) appoint ad hoc working groups drawn from higher education institutions, professional bodies and other stakeholders to advise the Commission;
(d) promote academic research on the use of AI in higher education;
(e) conduct periodic reviews to ensure that policies remain current and aligned with best practice; and
(f) require institutions to take corrective measures within prescribed timeframes where non-compliance is identified.
The regulations will also impose clear obligations on every higher education institution.
9. Cabinet has taken note of the status reached in the Local Government Reforms being undertaken with a view to conducting a thorough review of the entire local government system, so as to ensure that it reflects present realities and enables Local Authorities to respond more effectively to the needs of local communities. The Inter-Ministerial Committee under the chair of the Minister of Local Government met on four occasions and came up with proposals.
In order to ensure that the reform framework is inclusive, coherent and aligned with national priorities and constituency realities, consultations were held with all the elected members, both at local and national levels.
A Local Government Reform Cell has been set up at the level of the Ministry of Local Government. The Cell is currently analysing proposals received from the public and various organisations following the public consultation exercise. In addition, all views and comments received during the Consultative Workshop and the Consultative Meeting are currently being examined.
10. Cabinet has taken note of the setting up of an Inter-Ministerial Committee on the Price of Bread under the chair of the Minister of Commerce and Consumer Protection and comprising the Minister of Social Integration, Social Security and National Solidarity and the Minister of Industry, SME and Cooperatives.
11. Cabinet has taken note of complaints regarding alleged inappropriate behavior of Mr Frédéric Pavaday, a former music teacher of Loreto Junior School at Curepipe. The nature of the allegations relates to abusive conduct of the teacher against two students during their time at the school.
Senior officials of the Ministry of Education and Human Resource had a meeting with the President of the Parent Teacher Association and a parent on 24 February 2026 to discuss how the Ministry of Education and Human Resource can extend its support to the students and parents of the aggrieved parties. The Ministry of Education and Human Resource proposed to offer psychological assistance to students and parents requiring same.
The support of the Ministry of Education and Human Resource aims to ensure the emotional safety and well-being of students during this period of heightened public concern regarding child protection protocols, mandatory reporting, and institutional responsibilities in educational settings.
12. Cabinet has taken note of the decision of the Supreme Court in the matter of Okiyo Maritime Corp v State of Mauritius ipo Ferney Ltd, Sega Sail Charters Ltd, Louis Jean Jerome de Chasteauneuf, Guy Harel, Club Nautique de Pointe Désny, Sandy Point Land and Sea Pty Ltd delivered on 23 February 2026.
Okiyo Maritime Corp, the registered owner of the MV Wakashio had lodged a case before the Supreme Court for the registered owner to set up a limitation fund by way of bank guarantee issued in favour of the State of Mauritius, an order directing that the said limitation fund be jointly managed by the Applicant and the Respondent in order to settle claims admitted by the Applicant and the Respondent and/or as directed by a Court of law and such other Order or Orders that the Supreme Court may deem fit and reasonable in the circumstances. On 23 February 2026, in its judgment, the Supreme Court authorised the Applicant to set up a limitation fund only for the payment of claims not related to oil pollution damage and to set up a bank guarantee for the sum of Rs719,658,463.31.
The State of Mauritius will, therefore, not be able to make any claim in excess of the sum of Rs719,658,463.31 for any non-oil pollution damage resulting from the MV Wakashio oil spill.
13. Cabinet has taken note of the National Fintech Strategy Report which is aligned with the Financial Services Strategy 2025–2030 launched in July 2025. The Report sets out a comprehensive and coordinated framework to position fintech as a central driver of Mauritius’ digital economy and its future competitiveness as an International Financial Centre. It proposes
an integrated approach spanning over regulatory modernisation, digital-infrastructure readiness, talent development, innovation and investment facilitation, international market positioning and financial inclusion.
The Strategy Report was developed with the technical support from the United Nations Economic Commission for Africa and reviewed by a Technical Fintech Committee comprising representatives of relevant Ministries and regulatory bodies. The Strategy is structured around six mutually reinforcing pillars designed to build a future-ready, innovation led ecosystem capable of supporting the evolution of financial services in an increasingly complex and technology-driven global market.
The National Fintech Strategy Report also includes a coordinated policy, execution, and skills development so that Fintech becomes a core engine of digital‑economy growth. It recognises that the continued success of Mauritius as a financial centre requires greater agility, interoperability, innovation-readiness and trust-building measures in a rapidly evolving global financial landscape. The Strategy aims to provide a clear policy direction, execution pathway and skills-development agenda for the country to build a future-ready fintech ecosystem.
14. Cabinet has agreed to the Drains Infrastructure Construction Ltd proceeding with the implementation of the project “Flood Mitigation Measures in the region of Vallée des Prêtres under Phase 1 – Construction of floodwall along eastern boundary of River Latanier over a stretch of 240 metres near Morcellement Ramlagun”.
15. Cabinet has agreed to the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding between the Ministry of Health and Wellness, “La Fondation de la Société de Pneumologie de L’Océan Indien (SPOI), Paris, France” and “La Fondation de l’Académie de Médecine, Paris, France”.
The aim of the SPOI is to promote access to pulmonary care, particularly through the support and development of bronchoscopy to evaluate pulmonary fibrosis and assess interstitial lung diseases for proper treatment protocol for countries in the Indian Ocean region. The objectives of signing the Memorandum of Understanding are to:
(a) raise public awareness of the challenges faced by Indo-Oceanic populations;
(b) develop an interventional pulmonology service in each SPOI member country, including Mauritius, with the aim of promoting access to respiratory care to a greater number of patients;
(c) provide specialised equipment for diagnosis and treatment;
(d) provide capacity building through structured training of medical and paramedical staff; and (e) establish a sustainable service integrated with public hospitals.
16. Cabinet has agreed to the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding between the Trust Fund for Specialised Medical Care (TFSMC) and F. Hoffmann-La Roche Ltd (ROCHE) for collaboration to conduct a gap analysis on clinical trials at the National Cancer Centre in Mauritius. The objectives of the Memorandum of Understanding are, inter alia, to:
(a) establish a structured collaboration between the TFSMC and ROCHE so as to assess the current framework for conducting clinical trials in Mauritius, with focus on the National Cancer Centre as a clinical trial site;
(b) identify gaps in processes, infrastructure, regulatory and operational readiness for clinical trials and benchmark these against international standards, including Good Clinical Practice; and
(c) provide a framework, recommendations and an action plan to strengthen national capacity for clinical trials and support the development of clear standards adapted to the local context.
The proposed gap analysis would provide Government and relevant institutions with an evidence-based assessment of the current state of readiness for international clinical trials, together with prioritised recommendations tailored to the Mauritian context and aligned with international scientific and ethical standards.
17. Cabinet has agreed to the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding between the Ministry of Information Technology, Communication and Innovation and the International Institute of Information Technology, Bangalore for collaboration on Digital Public Infrastructure.
The Ministry of Information Technology, Communication and Innovation is currently pursuing the implementation and strengthening of its Digital Public Infrastructure to enhance secure digital service delivery, interoperability among public institutions and trusted digital transactions for citizens and businesses. Digital Public Infrastructure is the digital backbone on which e-government and digital economy services run.
18. Cabinet has agreed to Mauritius tabling its Initial Schedule of Commitments covering sectors under Business and Environmental Services in the context of the second round of Southern African Development Community Trade in Services negotiations.
19. Cabinet has agreed to private Higher Education Institutions providing Foundation Programmes for students, including international ones, who do not meet direct entry requirements for undergraduate studies, as is the case for public Higher Education Institutions. The Foundation Programmes will be introduced in private Higher Education Institutions subject to the approval of the Higher Education Commission in order to adapt the entry requirements to higher education to the secondary school exit qualifications across countries.
An accredited Foundation Programme is a pre-degree academic programme of a duration of one year or two semesters with four modules per semester with one module in English language in each semester. The programme is designed to prepare learners for entry into the first year of undergraduate degree programme in a specified field of study. A Foundation Programme is an integral component of a defined progression pathway within a Higher Education Institution.
20. Cabinet has taken note of the activities being organised in the context of World Obesity Day, which is observed annually on 04 March with the aim of raising awareness and advocating for solutions to the global obesity crisis. The theme for this year is “8 Billion Reasons to Act on Obesity.”
To commemorate World Obesity Day 2026, the Ministry of Health and Wellness will organise an official ceremony on 04 March 2026. The programme will comprise a keynote address and a panel discussion, with around 200 participants. In addition, the following activities will also be held:
(a) an Inter-College Poster Competition, in collaboration with the Ministry of Education and Human Resource, for students of Grades 7, 8 and 9 to raise awareness about obesity;
(b) Health and Physical Activity promotion events at Recreation Centres;
(c) radio talks and television programmes on MBC TV centred on the theme “8 Billion Reasons to Act on Obesity”;
(d) talks and sensitisation campaigns at Community Centres, Women Centres, Social Welfare Centres, schools and in urban and rural settings on the risk factors of obesity and preventive measures;
(e) distribution of informational pamphlets during screening activities; and
(f) an exhibition on healthy lifestyles and balanced diets organised in collaboration with relevant stakeholders.
21. Cabinet has taken note of the outcome of the recent participation of the Minister of Agro-Industry, Food Security, Blue Economy and Fisheries in the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) Conference entitled “Mobilising the private sector for sustainable trade and investment in Africa’s agrifood sector”, organised jointly by the Governments of Romania and Kenya in Nairobi, Kenya.
The Conference brought together high-level representatives from African governments, the private sector and international partners to discuss policy options to enhance trade and investment in the agrifood sector in Africa. Mauritius has been recognised by the OECD as a key partner in advancing sustainable development, trade, investment, and regional integration through the African Continental Free Trade Area. The Conference addressed challenges and opportunities for enhancing trade and investment in Africa’s agrifood systems.
The Minister intervened in the session on “Bringing Sustainable Investment and Finance into the Agrifood Sector in Africa,” and shared Mauritius’ experience in the development of the sector and highlighted the challenges that the country faced after transforming the sugar industry following the phasing-out of the Sugar Protocol. He showcased the evolution of the sugar industry transitioning into the cane industry, highlighted the challenges faced by Small Island Developing States and emphasised opportunities for youth and women in agriculture. The Minister had bilateral meetings with the OECD, the Australian Trade & Investment Commission and the International Seed Federation.
22. Cabinet has taken note of the outcome of the recent participation of the Attorney-General in the meeting of Commonwealth Law Ministers and Senior Officials in the Republic of Fiji. Commonwealth Law Ministers, Attorneys General and Senior Officials met to discuss legal issues of interest to Commonwealth member countries. The theme of the Meeting was “Anchoring Justice in a Changing Tide: Strengthening the Rule of Law for a Resilient Future”.
A number of topics were discussed during the meeting namely:
(a) Democratic Resilience and Participation;
(b) Justice and Artificial Intelligence;
(c) Maritime Boundaries and Sea Level Rise;
(d) Combating Misinformation and Disinformation in Electoral and Other Democratic Processes; and
(e) Challenges in Third-Party Funding of Civil Litigation in the Commonwealth and in Asset Recovery.
In the margins of the meeting, the Attorney-General attended side events, including one organised by the Government of Singapore on the United Nations Convention on International Agreements Resulting from Mediation (the Singapore Convention) adopted in 2019.
23. Cabinet has taken note of the recent participation of the Minister of Financial Services and Economic Planning in the Global Business Summit organised by the ETNow -Times Group in New Delhi. The Summit brought together global leaders, including Shri Narendra Modi, Prime Minster of India, and focused on economic transformation, investment, artificial intelligence, and global development trends.
The Minister participated in a high-level panel on translating trade agreements into economic outcomes and highlighted Mauritius’ role as a strategic bridge between India and Africa, as well as the strengths of the Mauritius International Financial Centre.
In light of the recent Indian Supreme Court ruling in the Tiger Global case, the Minister engaged with Indian authorities to clarify the implications for Mauritian operators. Meetings were held with the Revenue Secretary of India, and Chairman and senior officials of the Central Board of Direct Taxes.
24. Cabinet has taken note of the outcome of the recent participation of the Minister of Foreign Affairs, Regional Integration and International Trade in the 48th Ordinary Session of the Executive Council and the 39th Ordinary Session of the Assembly of the African Union (AU), held in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia on the theme “Ensuring Sustainable Water Availability and Safe Sanitation Systems to Achieve the Goals of Agenda 2063. »
The Minister intervened on agenda item “Peace and Security” to convey Mauritius’ gratitude for the AU’s consistent recognition of our sovereignty over the Chagos Archipelago, including Diego Garcia.
On the AU institutional reform, the Minister advocated for the operationalisation of the African Court of Justice as a cornerstone of respect for the rule of law, and insisted that reform must guarantee equitable representation, while respecting the particular circumstances of island States and smaller Member States.
On the sidelines of the AU Executive Council and Summit, the Minister had meetings with, inter alia, the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Ethiopia, the Minister for Foreign Affairs of Ghana, the Minister of International Relations of Botswana and the President of the Pan-African Parliament. The Minister also attended the following continental meetings:
(a) 35th Forum of Heads of State and Government of the African Peer Review Mechanism;
(b) Pre-Summit Meeting of the Committee of African Heads of State and Government on Climate Change;
(c) Inaugural Session of the Sub-Committee on the Implementation of the African Continental Free Trade Area;
(d) Italy-Africa Summit;
(e) Meeting with the Corporate Council on Africa; and
(f) Meeting with the Africa Peer Review Mechanism.

title: Nvidia Overtakes Apple as TSMC Largest Customer
text:
Nvidia Overtakes Apple as TSMC’s Largest Customer
Nvidia has now surpassed Apple to become the largest customer of semiconductor giant TSMC.
For more than a decade, Apple was TSMC’s most important client, enjoying priority access to the world’s most advanced manufacturing processes for its chips. However, the situation has now changed.
AI Boom Pushes Nvidia to the Top
This shift is not a coincidence. It is a direct result of the artificial intelligence boom. While Apple’s business depends on yearly device upgrades such as iPhones, iPads, and Macs, Nvidia’s demand comes from data centers and cloud service providers, and it continues to grow rapidly.
Nvidia’s AI accelerators, based on architectures like Blackwell and Hopper, are ordered in very large volumes. They are also more complex and more expensive to produce than Apple’s A-series processors. This has helped Nvidia increase its importance for TSMC.
In 2025, Nvidia accounted for 19% of TSMC’s revenue, reaching NT$726.97 billion (about 23.4 billion US dollars), more than double the NT$352.27 billion recorded in 2024.
Projections for 2026 suggest that Nvidia could generate around 33 billion US dollars in revenue for TSMC, representing about 22% of the company’s total income. In comparison, Apple is expected to contribute around 27 billion US dollars, or about 18%.
Apple will remain a key partner and will continue to have access to advanced nodes such as 2nm technology. However, it will no longer be the only company influencing TSMC’s expansion plans. Nvidia now has enough financial weight to play a major role in the investment and development decisions of the world’s leading chip manufacturer.

title: Archer to Add Starlink to Midnight Electric Air Taxi
text:
Powered-lift aircraft, such as electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) air taxis, are the FAA’s first new category of aircraft since helicopters were introduced in the 1940s. At least some of those state-of-the-art aircraft could have state-of-the-art internet.
Archer Aviation on Friday revealed plans to integrate and test SpaceX’s Starlink internet service on its flagship Midnight. The company envisions providing high-speed connectivity for the air taxi’s four passengers as they make short hops between airports, downtown areas, and event venues in cities such as New York, Miami, and Los Angeles.
Archer said Starlink, which is built to provide internet even in dense urban areas and locations with minimal cell service, is “uniquely suited” to air taxi operations over cities at about 1,500 feet agl. The integration would mark Starlink’s first on an eVTOL air taxi.
Passengers won’t be the only ones using the service. Archer said Starlink will be part of its “air taxi connectivity architecture,” handling communications between Midnight, its pilots, and engineers on the ground.
“Connectivity is a must-have feature for Midnight. Starlink is uniquely built to deliver it,” said Adam Goldstein, founder and CEO of Archer. “This industry-first collaboration will enable seamless, high-speed connectivity and essential amenities for our passengers and pilots.”
Starlink’s Spread
Starlink comprises a constellation of nearly 10,000 satellites—and growing—that orbit the Earth at about 550 kilometers. That’s far lower than geostationary internet satellites, which shortens the distance the data needs to travel and enables speeds comparable to ground-based broadband services.
Passengers on Starlink-equipped aircraft can stream video, make phone calls, play online games, and more, even during taxi, takeoff, and landing. A flat, electronically steered antenna on the aircraft automatically tracks multiple satellites at once, handing off between them as the plane moves. A supplemental type certificate (STC) is required for installation on each airframe.
Regional charter provider JSX was the first carrier to equip its aircraft with Starlink back in 2023. Business Insider in January reported that 30 others have announced deals to outfit their fleets. That includes United Airlines, which plans to add the service to its entire fleet and in October completed its first Starlink-equipped mainland flight using a Boeing 737-800.
Southwest in February became the latest Starlink airline customer, announcing plans to install the service on more than 300 aircraft by year’s end, with the first to enter commercial service this summer. Others include Lufthansa, Alaska Airlines, British Airways, and Emirates, as well as the flag carriers of France, Spain, Italy, Japan, Korea, and many more.
The system can be installed on a wide variety of aircraft with STCs, including airframes from Boeing, Airbus, Embraer, Textron Aviation, Gulfstream, and Bombardier. Starlink is working on additional STCs for the Boeing 767, Airbus A380, Dassault Falcon 10X, Pilatus PC-12, and more in 2026.
Archer’s Midnight could be the next added to that list.
Not Just Wi-Fi
Archer sees other applications for Starlink beyond improving the experience of Midnight passengers and pilots.
The company said it will work with Starlink to develop a “connectivity solution” that could help it build future autonomous variants. Though Midnight will be piloted at launch, Archer plans to transition to uncrewed operations later, with Boeing air taxi unit Wisk Aero serving as its exclusive autonomy provider. Boeing in 2023 made an investment to fund the integration of Wisk’s technology on Midnight.
The firm is also working with autonomous systems developer Anduril on a hybrid-electric defense variant and will supply its electric powertrain for Anduril’s autonomous Omen concept.
In January, Archer revealed that it will integrate Nvidia’s IGX Thor AI computing module into “future iterations of its aircraft programs.” Thor is designed for quick and accurate onboard computing for autonomous decision-making, advanced perception, and predictive operations. The companies also plan to develop new AI systems for autonomous operations, airspace integration, and pilot safety.
Archer intends to use Hawthorne Municipal Airport (KHHR)—which it acquired for $126 million in November—as a testbed for its AI technologies. Hawthorne is the planned operational hub of the firm’s Los Angeles network, which could ferry athletes and spectators between venues at the 2028 Summer Olympic Games.
Archer has additional partnerships with the organizers of the 2026 FIFA World Cup and Super Bowl LXI in 2027, which will also take place in Los Angeles, though Midnight is not expected to fly passengers at those events.

title: OpenAI Record Funding Is All About Google Rivals Joining Forces
text:
- OpenAI raised $110 billion, with Amazon and Nvidia joining as major investors.
- OpenAI's biggest corporate backers are all intense rivals of Google.
- Elon Musk's original reason for funding OpenAI is even more true now.
When Elon Musk committed big money to start OpenAI in late 2015, he wanted to create another AI company to stop Google from dominating this important field.
While a lot has changed since then, the reason OpenAI exists still holds: Google has been working toward this AI moment for over 25 years, and it's the beast to beat.
Take a look at the companies backing OpenAI, which raised a record $110 billion on Friday.
OpenAI's largest corporate investors are fierce Google rivals that will benefit greatly if OpenAI manages to seriously challenge Google in the battle for AI supremacy and control over how digital information flows in this new era.
It's become "everyone else against Google," in my view. Here's the breakdown:
Amazon is Google's biggest cloud rival
The new big kahuna. Amazon is investing $50 billion in OpenAI.
Amazon competes with Google in cloud computing. AI workloads have become the big new growth engine for this sector, and Google has been growing quickly.
Amazon also rivals Google in product search, one of the most valuable parts of the online search business. Google has woven AI throughout its search offering, and Amazon is behind in this area.
Google has been designing AI chips called TPUs (Tensor Processing Units) for about a decade. Amazon has a similar offering called Trainium—another huge area of competition.
Amazon's $50 billion investment in OpenAI gets it several goodies that can help it fight Google.
Amazon Web Services will serve as the exclusive third-party cloud distributor for OpenAI's Frontier business product, while jointly developing custom AI models for Amazon's applications.
OpenAI will also use significant Trainium compute capacity to support advanced AI workloads and expand business access to its models via AWS's cloud infrastructure.
Nvidia competes with Google's AI chips
Nvidia agreed to invest $30 billion in OpenAI in this latest round.
Nvidia is the clear leader in AI chips, with its GPUs. However, Google TPUs have gained ground recently, with many of Nvidia's biggest customers also agreeing to either rent or buy TPUs. This could eat into Nvidia's market share and might slow its growth, if TPUs really catch on.
For its new investment, Nvidia also gets new goodies to help it fend off the Google threat.
OpenAI is deepening its partnership with Nvidia by using massive new computing capacity to better train and run its most advanced AI models.
The new deal specifically centers on Nvidia's upcoming Vera Rubin systems and significantly increases the computing power available to run OpenAI models after they are trained. This is known as inference, and it's becoming the most important part of the AI chip wars.
Microsoft still owns a big chunk of OpenAI
Although Microsoft didn't invest in the latest round, it has been OpenAI's biggest investor for years. Even after skipping this round, Microsoft likely owns more than 20% of OpenAI already and has a massive cloud-computing deal with the startup.
Microsoft is Google's original arch-enemy. Microsoft dominated the tech world before Google rose up in the early 2000s and took the web by storm.
These days, Microsoft competes with Google in almost everything it does, including cloud computing, business software, and search. (Bing lags far behind Google in search market share, though it still makes good money for Microsoft.)
Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella is still trying to make Google dance, and OpenAI is key to this.
With Friday's new OpenAI funding round, Microsoft's existing deal with OpenAI won't change, including how they share revenue, handle intellectual property, define advanced AI, and Microsoft's exclusive rights to provide certain services.

title: MP Materials Plans $1 . 25 Billion Rare Earth Magnet Campus
text:
The only U.S. operator of a rare earth mine is expanding in North Texas with a billion-dollar bet on magnets.
Las Vegas-based MP Materials said Thursday that it will build a 120-acre manufacturing campus in Northlake, about 27 miles north of Fort Worth, on land owned by Ross Perot Jr.’s Hillwood. The Dallas Business Journal reported that the nearly $1.3 billion project, dubbed “10X,” is expected to begin testing operations in 2028.
The plant will rise near Harmonson Road and FM 156 in Denton County, less than 10 miles from MP’s existing 250,000-square-foot magnet facility at Hillwood’s AllianceTexas development, according to the outlet. That plant, known as Independence, produces about 1,000 metric tons of neodymium-iron-boron magnets annually and employs more than 160 people. The new campus aims to scale output to 10,000 metric tons per year.
The expansion follows a multibillion-dollar public-private partnership with the U.S. Department of Defense which plans to invest more than $700 million in preferred stock and warrants, positioning it as MP’s largest shareholder. The company also operates the country’s only functioning rare earth mining and processing site at Mountain Pass, California.
The North Texas project is part of a broader federal push to blunt China’s dominance in rare earth magnets, which are essential for electric vehicles, smartphones and defense systems such as guided missiles, according to the publication. China controls roughly 92 percent of global supply, according to a 2022 U.S. Department of Energy report.
MP’s growth in Texas is also tied to private-sector demand, as the company recently inked a $500 million partnership with Apple and expects production to ramp up for General Motors this year, with Apple-related manufacturing slated for 2027, CEO James Litinsky told the outlet.
To land the project, local and state officials assembled a hefty incentives package. Denton County approved a 10-year tax abatement covering 50 percent of real and personal property taxes, capped at $9.3 million. Northlake signed off on its own incentives, and the company secured more than $66 million combined from the Texas Enterprise Fund and the Texas Semiconductor Innovation Fund. Dallas-based CBRE led the national site search.
— Eric Weilbacher

title: The Trump phone sure looks a lot like this HTC handset
text:
Where’s the Trump phone? We’re going to keep talking about it every week. We’ve reached out, as usual, to ask about the Trump phone’s whereabouts, and have stopped getting a response. This week, thanks to a reader tip, we think we’ve found the original phone the T1 is based on.
The Trump phone sure looks a lot like this HTC handset
The 2024 HTC U24 Pro looks uncannily like our latest glimpse of the Trump phone.
The 2024 HTC U24 Pro looks uncannily like our latest glimpse of the Trump phone.
A long time ago, back when the Trump phone was but a single, inaccurate render and a contradictory spec sheet, we tried to figure out what other phone it might be based on. Now, eight months, two spec overhauls, and one redesign later, I have a good guess: the HTC U24 Pro.
I didn’t spot this by myself. A reader first tipped me off to the similarities between the U24 Pro and the Trump phone I revealed a few weeks ago, which are numerous: a similar angled body and curved display, a unique front sensor array, and broadly similar specs, including oddities like a headphone jack and microSD card support.
Let’s get one thing out the way at the top: I’m pretty sure HTC isn’t secretly building the Trump phone. Sohaib Ahmed, the company’s global director of PR, told me as much: “HTC does not design or manufacture phones for third parties.” But that doesn’t rule out another company playing a part in designing or building both phones, and given just how similar they look, I suspect that’s exactly what happened.
The U24 Pro is an odd phone in its own right. It launched in 2024 for €549 (around $600 at the time), and was heralded in some corners as a return from the dead for HTC, despite the fact that it followed the U23 Pro a year earlier, and a string of smaller releases that targeted the Asian market. HTC, once one of the major players in the Android phone industry, had been greatly diminished ever since selling a large part of its smartphone business to Google for $1.1 billion in 2017.
Both the T1 Phone and the U24 Pro share a slightly asymmetric angular frame, jutting out into a point along every edge, with a heavily curved screen on the front. I cannot think of, nor find, another phone that is quite the same shape — even small details like the positions of the antenna lines around the power and volume buttons line up. Even more telling is the set of sensors along each phone’s top. The U24 Pro splits up its speaker grill, notification LED, and proximity sensor into three, resulting in an unusual design with a long bar, a small dot for the LED, and then a second shorter bar, at the point where the display meets the phone’s top. What looks like the same sensor design can be clearly seen on the Trump phone I was shown, though I can’t confirm that the sensors themselves are the same, or that the T1 Phone will also have a notification LED.
Other details are subtler. It’s hard to tell exactly from the glimpses I got of the Trump phone, but along the top frame it certainly looks to have both a headphone jack and a microphone in the exact same positions as on the HTC device. Even having a headphone jack in the first place is evidence of the phones’ connection, given how rare the feature is in modern devices. The same can be said for the fact that both phones include a microSD card slot, sharing support for up to 1TB cards on top of a base 512GB of storage.
While the rear cameras look completely different, which seems to scupper my theory, look again: The U24 Pro’s cameras are split into a pair close together, with a third lens lower down. The new Trump phone sets all its cameras in one mount, but they’re unevenly spaced, with a larger gap between the second and third lenses. These cameras may look totally different on the surface, but I suspect that underneath you’ll find they’re similar. And what we know of the specs matches too: Trump Mobile told me that its phone will have 50-megapixel sensors for both the main and selfie cameras, and my glimpse of its camera UI showed an ultrawide and a 2x zoom option — all of which you’ll find on the U24 Pro.
Assuming that the two phones are essentially the same, does the U24 Pro tell us any more about what to expect from the T1? It’s powered by Qualcomm’s Snapdragon 7 Gen 3, matching what I’d been told about a Snapdragon 7-series chip in the Trump phone. The U24 Pro has a 6.8-inch OLED display, 60W wired charging and even wireless charging support, and an IP67 rating for dust and water protection. There’s reason to be cautious reading too much into the spec sheet, though: The U24 Pro released with a 4,600mAh battery, smaller than the 5,000mAh capacity I’ve been told will be in the Trump phone. If that spec differs, then other elements of the internals could well too — the bigger battery in particular might replace the wireless charging coils of the HTC phone.
The U24 Pro launched to middling reviews, which may not inspire much confidence in Trump Mobile’s offering. Tech Advisor gave the phone two and a half stars out of five, calling it “hard to recommend over rivals,” while Notebookcheck declared it “modern and yet out of date.” Still, it fared surprisingly well in a JerryRigEverything teardown and durability test.
He noticed, however, that this didn’t appear to be the HTC of old, lacking the sapphire crystal HTC previously used on its displays and optical image stabilization on the front camera it once pioneered. The U24 Pro and other HTC phones released in the last few years share little consistent design language, and ship with near-stock Android software. Speculation has been rife that HTC isn’t in fact manufacturing the phones internally, but had commissioned an ODM — original design manufacturer — to produce the hardware. ODMs design and manufacture phones for other companies, usually to a set of specifications provided by the consumer-facing brands, and often retain ownership of the designs, allowing them to reuse and repurpose them for other clients. I asked HTC for clarification, but the company wouldn’t confirm whether the U24 Pro was made in-house or contracted out.
It’s certainly possible that Trump Mobile, or its manufacturing partner, is simply ripping off the design of a little-known HTC device. But I think the most likely answer here is a more mundane one: HTC probably worked with some unnamed ODM to design and manufacture the U24 Pro, and Trump Mobile just happened to work with the same company. That manufacturer may have either dug out its old designs for HTC when developing the T1 Phone, or perhaps gave new life to old, unused components from HTC’s production run. Without knowing the hypothetical ODM involved, it’s hard to say for sure. Trump Mobile has previously confirmed that it’s working with a partner or partners to manufacture its phones outside the US, before their “final assembly” in Miami, but executive Don Hendrickson wouldn’t tell me the name of its manufacturer or where it’s based. I reached out to the company again for comment on this story, and received no reply.
So no, I don’t think that HTC made the Trump phone. But it does look likely that it is based, more or less, on one of that company’s phones. It remains to be seen how the T1 Phone will differ in its details, but the fact that its origins seem so closely tied to an unpopular midranger from two years ago doesn’t exactly inspire confidence in the T1 Phone’s flagship credentials.
Got inside information on Trump Mobile or the Trump phone? Reach out securely from a personal device to tips@theverge.com, or see our How to Tip Us page.

title: OpenAI finalizes $110 billion funding at $730 billion value
text:
By Shirin Ghaffary and Matt Day, Bloomberg
OpenAI has raised $110 billion in a deal that values the startup at $730 billion, representing the ChatGPT maker’s largest funding round to date and bolstering its costly push to secure more computing power and talent for AI development.
Amazon.com Inc. is investing $50 billion in the financing round, OpenAI said Friday, by far the largest amount the e-commerce giant has put into any company. Amazon will initially invest $15 billion, followed by another $35 billion in the coming months when certain conditions are met, OpenAI said.
SoftBank Group Corp. and Nvidia Corp. each invested $30 billion, OpenAI said. The firm’s new $730 billion valuation doesn’t include the money raised. Post-money, it’s now valued at $840 billion. OpenAI expects to bring on additional investors as the funding round progresses.
The deal, in the works for months, comes against the backdrop of growing concerns that AI developers and Big Tech firms are spending too much on data centers and chips for AI, with an uncertain payoff. OpenAI alone has previously said it’s committed to spend more than $1.4 trillion on AI infrastructure. To finance those bets, OpenAI and rival Anthropic PBC have increasingly tapped an overlapping group of venture funds and tech companies.
The large investment from Amazon, a longtime Anthropic backer, also tightens its relationship with OpenAI. As part of the agreement, OpenAI will use Amazon’s line of in-house AI chips, called Trainium, and jointly develop customized models for Amazon’s own engineering teams.
OpenAI will also spend an additional $100 billion on Amazon Web Services over the next eight years. The two companies in November announced a deal under which the model builder would use some $38 billion in AWS services over seven years.
“Amazon can deliver so much to us in terms of new demand and opportunities in the market,” OpenAI Chief Executive Officer Sam Altman said in an interview with CNBC on Friday. Andy Jassy, Amazon’s CEO, said the deal “will yield a good return for Amazon over a long period of time.”
OpenAI also plans to deploy 5 gigawatts worth of Nvidia’s Vera Rubin hardware for training and running its models, the chipmaker said in a statement.
Microsoft Corp., one of OpenAI’s largest previous backers and formerly an exclusive infrastructure partner, said its relationship with the developer remains strong. “Nothing about today’s announcements in any way changes the terms of the Microsoft and OpenAI relationship,” the companies said in a joint statement Friday.
Anthropic raised $30 billion in a funding round earlier this month from investors, including Nvidia and Microsoft. The financing valued Anthropic at $380 billion, including the money raised.
The funding commitments mark the latest example of circular financing deals in which chipmakers and cloud providers back the leading AI startups who are also their customers. These tie-ups are intended to ensure the AI sector can meet its immense infrastructure needs, but the risk is such deals can magnify losses if demand for AI fails to match today’s lofty expectations.
Altman downplayed the risk of such arrangements in the CNBC interview.
“I get where the concern comes from,” Altman said. “This only makes sense if new revenue flows into the whole AI ecosystem.” He said much of his effort goes into trying to get more computing capacity to serve demand for ChatGPT and OpenAI’s other products.

title: OpenAI announces $110 billion investment round at $730 billion pre - money valuation
text:
OpenAI announces $110 billion investment round at $730 billion pre-money valuation
NVIDIA pitched in $30 billion, another $30 billion came from Softbank and $50 billion came from Amazon.
OpenAI has announced a new round of investment funding this week with a pre-money valuation of $730 billion set for the company. In this round, OpenAI announced that NVIDIA, Softbank, and Amazon were its big investors, pitching in a total of $110 billion to aid Sam Altman’s company in building out its AI infrastructure as one of the largest AI software and model engineers in the industry right now.
The company announced its latest funding round in a press release on the OpenAI website this week.
Helping AI reach more people requires deep collaboration across the ecosystem.
— OpenAI (@OpenAI) February 27, 2026
Today we’re announcing new investment, with support from @SoftBank, @NVIDIA, and @Amazon, to scale the infrastructure needed to bring AI to everyone.https://t.co/xW0ItgMTLe
There, OpenAI discloses the total financed by its investing partners:
The $30 billion from NVIDIA is notable, as a rumored $100 billion deal between NVIDIA and OpenAI has been a will-the-won’t-they topic of discussion among investors and analysts, and while NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang backed away from talking about it, recent activity shows NVIDIA still intends to be a lucrative investment partner for Sam Altman’s AI company.
As OpenAI still expects more investors to engage in its latest investment round, stay tuned for more updates and news on the OpenAI topic.

title: Rakuten Mobile to deploy 3 , 000 Open mMIMO radios in Japan
text:
Sudhakar Pandey, head of RAN at Rakuten Mobile, told RCR Wireless News that the telco is expecting around 7% throughput gain in high traffic areas with the wider coverage due to higher gain antennas
In sum – what to know:
3,000 radios in first phase – Rakuten Mobile is installing around 3,000 32T32R massive MIMO O-RAN radios as part of a broader rollout strategy.
7% throughput gain expected – The operator anticipates roughly 7% higher throughput in high-traffic areas due to wider coverage and higher gain antennas.
Smooth O-RAN integration – The 1Finity MIMO radios integrated without major challenges, with configurations largely aligned to O-RAN specifications.
Japanese carrier Rakuten Mobile plans to deploy around 3,000 32T32R massive MIMO O-RAN radios as part of the first phase of a broader rollout, the operator confirmed in an interview with RCR Wireless News.
Sudhakar Pandey, head of RAN at Rakuten Mobile, said: “Rakuten Mobile plans to deploy around 3,000 radios in the first phase of a larger deployment. We are expecting around 7% throughput gain in high traffic areas with the wider coverage due to higher gain antennas.”
The deployment uses 1Finity massive MIMO radios and is focused on improving performance in dense, high-traffic locations. According to Pandey, the integration with Rakuten’s existing O-RAN infrastructure has been straightforward.
“It was a very smooth integration. The 1finitiy MIMO integration didn’t face any challenge due to the majority of the configuration were already aligned with the O-RAN specifications,” he said, referring to alignment with O-RAN standards and Rakuten Symphony’s CU/DU architecture.
Pandey added that the rollout supports the operator’s broader 5G expansion strategy. “The deployment of 1Finity radios is part our 5G expansion across Japan, specifically in high density areas.”
Earlier this month, Rakuten Mobile and 1Finity, a Fujitsu company, had announced a collaboration to deploy 1Finity’s massive MIMO O-RAN radios across Rakuten Mobile’s nationwide 5G network in Japan.
The Japanese telco also noted that 1Finity massive MIMO radios are based on Qualcomm’s Dragonwing QRU100 platforms and are designed to meet O-RAN specifications. According to the companies, the passively cooled radio units are intended to support sustainable network expansion while maintaining high energy efficiency and long-term operational reliability.
Last year, the Japanese carrier had named Cisco, Nokia, and F5 as its lead technology partners for the deployment of its 5G Standalone (SA) network. The Japanese company noted that these alliances will “significantly enhance its network capabilities, simplify operations through AI-driven systems, and drive innovation” across Japan.
The initial phase centers on capacity enhancement in urban zones, where traffic loads are highest, with further deployment expected as part of the telco’s wider 5G network evolution.

title: US stocks sink and oil prices rise as worries about AI , inflation and possible war hit Wall Street
text:
By STAN CHOE AP Business Writer
NEW YORK (AP) — U.S. stocks are sinking Friday as Wall Street gets back to hunting companies that could become losers because of the artificial-intelligence revolution. A surprisingly discouraging update on inflation is also hurting the market, while oil prices climb with worries about tensions between the United States and Iran.
The S&P 500 fell 0.7% and is staggering toward the finish of what would be just its second losing month in the last 10. The Dow Jones Industrial Average was down 727 points, or 1.5%, as of 12:38 p.m. Eastern time, and the Nasdaq composite was 0.9% lower.
The losses came as investors returned to knocking down software companies and others whose businesses they suspect could get supplanted by AI-powered competitors.
Block, the company behind Cash App, Square and other businesses, gave a potential signal of what AI could do after CEO Jack Dorsey said he was cutting its workforce by nearly half. That’s even though he said 2025 was a strong year for the company, which is sending more cash to shareholders through stock buybacks.
“Intelligence tools have changed what it means to build and run a company,” Dorsey said in a letter to investors while announcing Block’s latest profit results. “We’re already seeing it internally. A significantly smaller team, using the tools we’re building, can do more and do it better.”
The co-founder of Twitter also said, “I don’t think we’re early to this realization. I think most companies are late. Within the next year, I believe the majority of companies will reach the same conclusion and make similar structural changes.”
Block is cutting more than 4,000 jobs from its workforce of over 10,000. Its stock jumped 14.4%.
Capable AI tools that can replace humans could also replace entire companies, or at least eat away at their profit margins. Fears about AI disruption have been causing sudden and swift sell-offs for stocks seen as potentially under threat, rolling through industries as different as trucking logistics and legal services.
Salesforce, whose platform helps customers manage their relationships with clients, fell 2.5%. It gave back most of its 4% gain from the day before after reporting a better profit than analysts expected.
A widely followed ETF tracking the software industry, meanwhile, sank 2% to bring its loss for the year so far to 23.4%.
The pain has also filtered out to private-equity companies that have lent money to software companies, which need to withstand the AI threat to keep repaying their debt. Blue Apollo Global Management dropped 8.7%, and Ares Management sank 7.8% for two of the biggest losses in the S&P 500.
Even the companies currently seeing their revenue and profit soar because of AI-related demand are weakening. Nvidia fell 2.6% and was the heaviest weight on the U.S. stock market, a day after dropping to its worst loss since last spring. That’s even though it reported a better profit than analysts expected and forecast more in revenue for the current quarter.
Rival chip companies also fell. Worries are hurting such companies not only about whether their stock prices rose too high in recent years but also whether the huge spending driving their growth can continue. Can big spenders like Amazon and Alphabet make back all their billions of dollars in AI investments through higher productivity and profits in the future?
On the winning side of Wall Street was Netflix, which climbed 12% after walking away from its bid to buy Warner Bros. Discovery’s studio and streaming business. That put Skydance-owned Paramount in a position to take over its Hollywood rival.
Paramount Skydance shares jumped 22.1%, while Warner Bros. Discovery fell 2%.
Some of the strongest action in financial markets was for oil, where the price for a barrel of benchmark U.S. crude oil rose 2.2% to $66.66. It’s the latest swing in a market unsettled by tensions between the United States and Iran over Iran’s nuclear program.
The U.S. military has already gathered a massive fleet of aircraft and warships in the Middle East, and a conflict could disrupt the global flow of oil and drive prices higher.
Brent crude, the international standard, rose 2.2% to $72.43 per barrel.
Also hurting the broad market was a report showing that inflation at the U.S. wholesale level was at 2.9% last month, much higher than the 1.6% that economists expected.
The number was so much worse than expected that it could help persuade the Federal Reserve to hold off longer on its cuts to interest rates. Lower rates would give the economy and prices for investments a boost, but they risk worsening inflation at the same time.
In the bond market, the yield on the 10-year Treasury was at 3.97%. It briefly swiveled higher following the inflation report, but it’s down from its 4.02% level late Thursday. Treasury yields often fall when nervousness is high and investors are moving into investments that are considered safer.
ln stock markets abroad, indexes were mixed in Europe and Asia. South Korea’s Kospi fell 1% from its latest record, and the United Kingdom’s FTSE 100 rose 0.6% in two of the world’s larger moves.
___
AP Business Writers Matt Ott and Elaine Kurtenbach contributed.

title: Man admits dangerous driving that killed work mate and left woman with life altering injuries
text:
Man admits dangerous driving that killed work mate and left woman with life altering injuries
A man who admitted dangerous driving that killed his work colleague and left another woman with life altering injuries in a head on collision in Louth four years ago said he 'deeply regrets what happened every day'.
A man who admitted dangerous driving causing the death of his work colleague and leaving another motorist with life-altering injuries in a devastating head-on collision in Co Louth four years ago said he will carry the consequences of his actions “for the rest of my life.”
Adam Doherty (29), of Foxhill in Derry, has offered what his counsel described as a “sincere and heartfelt apology” and “deeply regrets what happened every day”. He is due to be sentenced at a later date.
Dundalk Circuit Court heard on Friday, February 27 at a sentencing hearing that Doherty was driving home from a night shift at Intel in Leixlip with his two work colleagues, Aaron Deery (27) and Cian Kennedy (26), when the fatal crash occurred shortly before 5am on the N33 near Ardee on July 22, 2022.
The court heard that Doherty, who has no previous convictions, said he attempted to overtake another vehicle but, after pulling out, was suddenly confronted by an oncoming car directly in his path. He was unable to avoid the impact.
“I tried to avoid the vehicle. It was an accident,” he told gardai, the court heard. The force of the collision claimed the life of front-seat passenger Mr Deery, also from Derry, and left back-seat passenger Cian Kennedy injured.
The driver of the Volkswagen Polo that Doherty’s Vauxhall Grandland struck, Leah Watters (24), suffered catastrophic injuries, including a fractured skull, two brain haemorrhages, a neck injury, a fractured left foot and a deep laceration to her knee.
The court heard she had to be resuscitated in hospital and continues to undergo medical treatment as a result of the crash.
Sergeant Evan Connolly told prosecuting counsel Miska Hanahoe that Doherty’s vehicle had crossed lanes and impacted the vehicle Ms Watters was driving.
The court heard Doherty and his two work colleagues started work at 6pm at Intel and left work at 4am, an hour earlier than usual.
Det Gda Evans said Ms Watters told gardai she was driving home after working as a DJ and all she remembers is “seeing lights coming towards me” before walking up in hospital.
Mr Kennedy, the rear seat passenger of the Vauxhall Grandland said he was asleep and woke up and saw glass. He saw Mr Deery ‘slumped over in the front seat’ and heard gurgling from Doherty, who then fell out of the driver’s side door.
Mr Kennedy said he had severe pain in his legs and couldn’t find a pulse for Mr Deery.
A motorist came on the scene and told gardai there was extensive damage to the Volkswagen Polo and the Vauxhall was ‘planted in the hedge’. The motorist discovered Doherty lying beside the car and then ‘stumbling around.’
The motorist said Ms Watters was ‘screaming in pain and there was blood on her face.’ He rang emergency services.
Another motorist arrived on the scene and told gardai he comforted Ms Watters, and recounted seeing Doherty ‘stumbling around.’ He said Mr Kennedy told him he couldn’t move in the back seat and the motorist asked the front seat passenger Mr Deery if he was ok.
He said Mr Deery ‘let out a breath,’ but was then not responsive and had no pulse. Mr Deery was pronounced dead in hospital, the court heard.
Gardai breathalysed Doherty at the scene and he passed the test, the court heard. The weather was good and the road was dry, the court also heard.
In a victim impact report read out on behalf of Mr Deery’s mother Maureen, who attended court supported by family members, Ms Deery said: “When Aaron died, his loss completely changed my life. There is an unimaginable pain I carry every single day.
“His absence is felt every single day. The grief has taken its toll on me physically and mentally and without my family I don’t think I’d be here today.
“My pain stems solely from the loss of my son. I am no longer the person I was. The trauma, grief and sadness never leave me. I never felt anger towards the driver involved.”
Ms Watters’ victim impact report was also read out. She said the collision “changed my life forever".
“I am in chronic pain and it affects my daily routine. I am still getting treatment. My whole life revolved around football and the gym and that has been taken away from me.
“My whole personality has changed. I am uneasy and anxious and have panic attacks and night terrors. It has broken me in a way I never imagined,” Ms Watters said, adding she is unable to use her degree in Sports & Science because “my body is unfit for that line of work.”
“My brain trauma has made further studies impossible,” she said. Defence counsel said Doherty has offered a “sincere and heartfelt apology” and “deeply regrets what happened every day.”
Judge Dara Hayes offered his deepest condolences to Ms Deery and her family and told them “I am very sorry for your loss. Nothing I can say will fix that and I am sorry you are here under these circumstances.”
The judge also told Ms Watters he is also very sorry she is here in court under these circumstances and “the significant injuries have changed the course of your life.”
The judge adjourned finalisation of sentencing Doherty until May 7 for a psychiatric report and assessment on Doherty.

title: General Hospital Spoilers : Nathan Is Sidwell PCPD Informant , Secretly Leaking Intel
text:
General Hospital (GH) spoilers reveal that Jenz Sidwell (Carlo Rota) recently told Drew Cain (Cameron Mathison) quite a story as he explained how Drew ended up in his current locked-in state.
During Sidwell’s lengthy and highly entertaining explanation, he divulged something that could lead to a big reveal involving another character.
In GH’s February 26 episode, Sidwell mentioned that with the help of inside info from his informants at the PCPD, he became convinced Willow Cain (Katelyn MacMullen) was the one who shot Drew.
That’s why Sidwell felt confident Willow would be receptive to his offer, which involved access to the paralytic that made it seem like Drew had a stroke.
Willow was indeed on board, so that led to this Sidwell partnership and the drug doses that keep Drew locked in.
However, we want to focus on the part about Sidwell’s informants at the PCPD.
We have to consider who was supplying inside information related Willow’s trial and helping Sidwell figure out that Willow was guilty.
Could Nathan West (Ryan Paevey) be one of those informants?
After all, Nathan got involved in Drew’s case and helped Dante Falconeri (Dominic Zamprogna) along the way.
Nathan was right there with Dante when they watched the incriminating traffic cam footage, so maybe he gave Sidwell that news soon after and pointed the finger at Willow.
It’s not like there’s a long line of people at the PCPD who would’ve had access to evidence against Willow.
It makes sense that Nathan leaked intel to Sidwell and that he’s perhaps been positioned there so that Sidwell always stays a few steps ahead of the cops.
Despite the DNA proof, matching fingerprints and missing corpse, there’s no guarantee this Nathan is the real deal.
It feels like there’s some trickery going on here, even if it’s just a matter of another man essentially living in Nathan’s body.
Nathan may have the memories of someone else depending on where the writers are going with this storyline. T
here are definitely times when it seems like Nathan isn’t quite the Nathan that fans used to know.
With that in mind, Nathan may be keeping some big secrets about his connection to Sidwell, Ross Cullum (Andrew Hawkes) and Cesar Faison’s (Anders Hove) final project.
We can’t forget how Nathan popped up that night after Laura Collins (Genie Francis) found Professor Henry “Hank” Dalton (Daniel Cosgrove) in her trunk.
What was Nathan doing out in that secluded area? Didn’t it seem like someone – namely Sidwell – perhaps ordered Nathan to be there?
It’s possible Nathan was involved in planting Dalton’s body, gathering blackmail photos or even just providing a police presence to rattle Laura as well as Sonny Corinthos (Maurice Benard).
Bottom line, Sidwell definitely has police informants based on what he told Drew, so Nathan seems like a good contender for one of Sidwell’s sources at the PCPD.
General Hospital spoilers say Sidwell will keep those shockers coming, so stay tuned!
CDL’s where you want to be for terrific General Hospital spoilers and news, so make us your go-to GH source.

title: Where Will Rigetti Computing Be in 5 Years ?
text:
After Rigetti Computing (RGTI 1.76%) rode a wave of enthusiasm that saw the quantum computing stock reach above $58, shares have tumbled to around $16 as investors question whether the company's sky-high valuation is justified.
Some recent stumbles have accelerated the stock's decline, like the delay of Rigetti's most ambitious quantum computer to date, Cepheus-1-108Q, and its failure to advance in a crucial government program.
While Rigetti bulls point to partnerships with chipmaking giant Nvidia and other government agencies as signs of a bright future, headlines and potential only go so far. Rigetti needs to start turning that potential into real dollars.
NASDAQ: RGTI
Key Data Points
The Rigetti bull case
In the best case, over the next few years, Rigetti makes real breakthroughs in its technology, scales significantly, and starts landing the kind of contracts that really move the needle -- not one-off orders worth a few million dollars, but sustained commercial relationships worth tens of millions annually. By 2031, revenue reaches north of $100 million, margins improve, and the path to profitability is clear.
In that scenario, you could see the stock trading at two to three times today's levels. Rigetti has some real ingredients working in its favor, but a whole lot has to go right for this to come true -- and fast.
Rigetti and other quantum stocks have a problem
Useful quantum computing has been "five years away" for a lot longer than that. The industry has made real progress, yes, but the gap between lab breakthroughs and useful commercial products remains enormous, and that gap is everything.
Rigetti has to compete with not only better-funded peers but also the likes of Alphabet, IBM, and Microsoft. These companies have R&D budgets that exceed Rigetti's entire market cap many times over. If it takes much longer than five years, Rigetti could be in trouble.
The company's more than $450 million in cash buys years of runway -- about five at current burn rates -- but that is if nothing changes. Expenses may ramp considerably as the company attempts to stay in the race and keep up with its competition. The company will need to raise more capital, likely a lot, and that could dilute existing shareholders considerably -- as it already has.
Here's what I think the next five years hold for Rigetti
The most probable five-year outcome, in my view, is that technological breakthroughs will continue, but viable commercial computing will still be "just around the corner." In that time, revenue has grown, but not nearly enough.
I don't have a crystal ball, and I'm certainly not a technical expert in the field, so I can't say for certain when -- or if -- commercially viable quantum computing will arrive. I can say, however, that there is far too much uncertainty and, frankly, hype for me to invest when the company is already valued at $5.5 billion, and its stock is trading at more than 600 times sales.
I'd rather get my quantum exposure through a company that doesn't need quantum to work in the next few years -- or ever -- to justify its stock price, like Alphabet.

title: Why Nvidia insights suggest ASX tech shares are undervalued
text:
ASX tech shares have been sold off heavily over the last several months amid AI-related concerns. I think some of them are now significantly undervalued. Comments by the Nvidia Corp (NASDAQ: NVDA) boss – one of the most well-placed to judge the potential impacts of AI – suggest the (ASX) tech share space has gone down too much.
Nvidia is one of the most important businesses in the technology supply chain, providing chips needed for AI and data centres. AI companies like OpenAI and Anthropic wouldn't be able to do what they do without the foundations provided by Nvidia.
Let's take a look at what Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said and how it could be applied to ASX tech shares.
The markets got it wrong
Earlier this week, Huang spoke with CNBC's Becky Quick and shared his thoughts on investor concerns that AI agents might affect the earnings of several software companies.
He said that he thought "the markets got it wrong" regarding fears that AI agents will cannibalise the enterprise software industry. He doesn't think AI agents will replace the software tools, but will use them instead to boost efficiency. Huang then said to CNBC:
That's the reason why we also say agents are tool users.
All of these tools that we use today, whether it's Cadence or Synopsys or ServiceNow or SAP, these tools exist for a fundamentally good reason. These agentic AI will be intelligent software that uses these tools on our behalf and help us be more productive.
Nobody's going to service better than ServiceNow, and they're going to come up with agents that are really fine-tuned and optimized for the work that uses the tools that they have.
In the end, we need the tools to finish their work and put the information back in a way that we can understand.
I'm not in a position to know how AI tools and their use will develop in the coming years, but I think it would be too bearish to assume businesses will lose a large chunk of clients and margins in the next few years.
Why I think the ASX tech shares are undervalued
A share price is meant to reflect the long-term future prospects of a business, but I don't think the prospects of names like Xero Ltd (ASX: XRO), TechnologyOne Ltd (ASX: TNE), Pro Medicus Ltd (ASX: PME), Siteminder Ltd (ASX: SDR), REA Group Ltd (ASX: REA), and CAR Group Ltd (ASX: CAR) have dropped by 40% or 50%.
I think all of them have stronger economic moats than what the market is giving them credit for. Virtually all of them delivered strong revenue growth in the most recent reporting season, and I believe that profit margin improvement is quite likely to rise at many of them over the rest of FY26 as well.
I'm not sure I could commit to all of them for 50 years, but I'd be excited to buy any of them for my portfolio as a medium-term investment.

title: Is AMD a Buy After Meta Deal ? | The Motley Fool
text:
Shares of Advanced Micro Devices (AMD +1.23%) got a lift after the company agreed to a deal with social media giant Meta Platforms (META 1.91%). The stock is now about breakeven for 2026, while it's nearly doubled over the past year.
Under the terms of the multi-year deal, Meta has committed to buying 6 gigawatts of AMD's graphics processing units (GPUs) across multiple generations of its chips. The two companies are also expanding their partnership for central processing units (CPUs), with Meta being a lead customer of AMD's 6th-generation EPYC CPU.
As part of the deal, AMD has also issued warrants to Meta for up to 160 million of its shares. The warrants will vest with each gigawatt of GPU shipments, and are also tied to AMD's stock price. The deal appears to be structured very similarly to the one it struck with OpenAI, also for 6 gigawatts of GPUs, in October.
Is AMD stock a buy?
Between its deals with OpenAI and now Meta Platforms, AMD has commitments for 12 gigawatts of GPUs over the next several years. Based on Nvidia GPU pricing, each gigawatt of chips is worth about $35 billion. Even if AMD is selling its chips at a discount, this is a massive opportunity. The company generated just under $35 billion in total revenue in 2025, so these deals combined are likely worth more than 10 times its revenue last year.
With both Meta and OpenAI committing to using AMD GPUs, it also opens the door for other large hyperscalers (owners of massive data centers) to do the same. In fact, there were earlier reports that Microsoft had been developing toolkits to translate CUDA code for use with AMD's GPUs to help reduce inference costs. Nvidia's CUDA software moat isn't as wide for inference, so this growing market is a big opportunity for AMD.
Meanwhile, AMD has made it clear that it's willing to give away warrants for its stock in exchange for large deal commitments. This is essentially a deal to buy 5 gigawatts' worth of chips and get 1 free through an investment in the company. It's not dissimilar to wireless carriers giving away free phones to lock in customers.
NASDAQ: AMD
Key Data Points
Given AMD's distant No. 2 market share, it's actually not a bad strategy, as it incentivizes large customers to help make it successful. And I wouldn't think of this just at the GPU level, but on the CPU level, as well, as the CPU market is going to become increasingly important in the age of agentic AI. CPUs are likely to become a big AI growth driver in the coming years, and this is also a way for AMD to help maintain its market share lead in this area.
In light of its growing AI inference and CPU agentic AI opportunities, I'd buy AMD stock even after this jump in price.
